ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Singaporean/Malaysian chilli crab; FOODON_03302051 (crab dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans, cooked — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for stir-fried crab dish with sauce
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for chilli crab or Zi Char-style crab; A0CJW (cooked crustaceans) is the closest defensible code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific prepared crab dish.

serving.description: 1 whole crab dish, typically shared by 1–2 persons
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Zi Char-style chilli crab is approximately 600 g representing one whole crab with sauce, typically shared between one to two diners at a Malaysian or Singaporean seafood restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Crustaceans, crab, blue, cooked, moist heat (FDC ID 175180); supplemented with FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia for sauce component estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service; Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations / INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Crab flesh macros anchored to USDA FDC cooked blue crab (FDC ID 175180): protein ~18 g/100g raw flesh, fat ~1.1 g/100g raw flesh. Sauce contribution (chilli, tomato, egg, soy sauce, cooking oil) estimated using FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia (2nd edition) for regional condiment and sauce components. Final per-100g composite values represent the dish at approximately 60% crab flesh and 40% sauce by weight, yielding energy 125 kcal, protein 10 g, fat 7.5 g, carbohydrate 5 g per 100 g. Input values from the originating record (confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against these approved sources and found broadly consistent; approved-source adapted values are used as the primary basis.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate for mixed seafood dish with sauce
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chilli crab or Zi Char-style crab dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 35 (low) based on the analogous category of mixed seafood dishes with sauce as documented in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283), which is indexed in the University of Sydney GI Database. The primary carbohydrate contributors are cornstarch thickener and tomato-chilli sauce present in small quantities (5 g carbohydrate per 100 g); high protein content (10 g per 100 g) further attenuates glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per 100 g = (35 × 5) / 100 = 1.75. Glycaemic load per 600 g serving = (35 × 30) / 100 = 10.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg per 100 g from soy sauce, chilli paste, and tomato sauce
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced soy sauce and sambal, as a 600 g shared serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, approaching the daily limit of 2000 mg recommended by the Health Promotion Board Singapore.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5 g per 100 g) and high protein (10 g per 100 g) with estimated GI of 35
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low carbohydrate content and high protein from crab flesh moderate postprandial glucose response; patients with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor total carbohydrate from accompanying rice rather than from the crab dish itself, and request minimal cornstarch thickener.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg per 100 g and cholesterol 50 mg per 100 g; saturated fat is low at 1.67 g per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Crab flesh provides lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids beneficial for cardiovascular health, but the high sodium from condiments poses a risk for blood pressure management; patients with cardiovascular disease should share the dish across at least two persons and request lighter seasoning to reduce sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Crab provides omega-3 fatty acids and zinc; ginger and garlic provide anti-inflammatory polyphenols
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Stir-fried crab with ginger and garlic is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to anti-inflammatory seafood and spice components, but individuals sensitive to nightshades (tomato, chilli) should monitor their response and request reduced chilli paste.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chilli Crab (Zi Char Style), locally known as 辣椒螃蟹 (La Jiao Pang Xie) in Chinese and Ketam Cili in Malay, is a signature Malaysian and Singaporean seafood dish prepared in the Zi Char (煮炒) tradition — a style of Chinese-influenced hawker and restaurant cooking that emphasises wok-fired dishes served family-style. The dish features a whole fresh crab, typically mud crab or flower crab, stir-fried at high heat in a wok with a rich, semi-thick sauce made from fresh chillies, tomato sauce, fermented soybean paste, garlic, ginger, egg, and cornstarch. The sauce is savoury, mildly spicy, and slightly sweet, with a glossy, clingy texture that coats the crab shell and flesh. Variants include black pepper crab (with cracked black pepper and butter) and salted egg crab (with salted egg yolk sauce). Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 125 kcal per 100 g, with 10 g protein from crab flesh, 7.5 g fat primarily from cooking oil, and only 5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making it a relatively low-carbohydrate, high-protein seafood dish. Sodium is elevated at 300 mg per 100 g due to soy sauce and chilli paste. The dish is typically served as a shared centrepiece at a meal alongside steamed rice or fried mantou (Chinese buns), and is a staple of Malaysian Zi Char restaurants and Singaporean seafood restaurants. Clinically, it is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes patients due to low estimated GI (~35) and low carbohydrate content, but requires sodium monitoring for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients.
common_user_queries: Is chilli crab suitable for diabetics?
Is chilli crab high in cholesterol?
Chilli crab vs black pepper crab nutrition

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central (cooked blue crab, FDC ID 175180) and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia for sauce component estimation. The input record values (confidence 0.7) are broadly consistent with these approved-source estimates. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source provides an exact per-100g nutrient profile for this composite Zi Char-style chilli crab dish; values represent an adapted estimate from two approved sources.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for composite chilli crab dish (crab flesh plus sauce) is not available as a single unified entry in USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 175180) or FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table as of 2026-04-17. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not reported as a unified value in the primary approved sources consulted (USDA FDC ID 175180; FAO/INFOODS SEA). vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite Zi Char-style crab dish are not available as a unified entry in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA as of 2026-04-17. trans_fat_g reported as 0.03 g per 100g from input data; this is a trace level consistent with cooking oil use and is retained as reported. The dish name 'Dum Sum' in the input record appears to be a restaurant or menu trade name; the canonical food identity has been corrected to 'Chilli Crab (Zi Char Style)' as the most representative dish description. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) indexed in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for chilli crab exists. Halal status is marked false as crab is considered haram in strict Shafi'i Islamic jurisprudence followed in Malaysia and Singapore, though some schools permit it; this should be verified per the specific restaurant's halal certification. Nutrient source updated from iteration 2: replaced HPB Singapore Foodo reference (which was flagged as a forbidden source URL foodo.sg) with FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table as the approved secondary source for sauce component estimation alongside USDA FDC ID 175180.
